method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
No source documents were provided, so I can’t write a properly cited “Flora and fauna” section using the required document-number citation format. Please provide the numbered documents, and I’ll draft the section with citations for every sentence.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants, and consequently has no resident labor force, local market, or conventional island economy.[1] Historically, its main exploitable resource was guano; the United States claimed the island in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act, but the deposits are now exhausted.[1] The island briefly had strategic transport significance after colonization from Hawaii in 1935, serving as a planned aviation stop on routes between Hawaii and Australia.[1] In the present day, Howland is administered primarily for conservation as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] Marine resources in the wider U.S. Pacific Remote Island Areas, which include Howland, are associated with limited pelagic fishing activity; one 2012 fisheries-related assessment reported that these areas accounted for about 2 percent of relevant deployments in the U.S. exclusive economic zone categories it examined.[4]

==Time zone==
Howland Island is in the UTC−12:00 time zone, corresponding to nautical time zone Y (Yankee), along with Baker Island among the United States Minor Outlying Islands.[3] The island is uninhabited, and public entry is restricted by special permit.[4][5] Because of its UTC−12:00 offset, Howland is among the last places on Earth where a calendar date remains valid.[2] The “Anywhere on Earth” deadline convention treats a date as ending when it has ended in the UTC−12:00 zone, with Howland and Baker identified as the latest places where that date applies.[2] In global date-change observances, the day technically ends one hour later on Howland and Baker than in the last inhabited places, such as Niue and American Samoa.[5] The time difference between Howland Island and parts of Kiribati can be 26 hours.[1]

==History==
Archaeological evidence has been interpreted as indicating an early Polynesian presence on Howland Island before its appearance in Euro-American maritime records.[4]

The first recorded sightings and charting by American whaling vessels occurred as early as 1822; Nantucket captain George Worth sighted the island that year and named it Worth Island.[2][4] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship Isabella visited the island and gave it the name Howland, reportedly after the ship’s lookout.[2][4] The United States claimed Howland in 1857, together with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2]

Guano exploitation began in the late 1850s, when Netcher’s United States Guano Company attempted to take possession in 1859, although the American Guano Company was already operating on the island.[4] A court settled the dispute between the two companies in 1865 by allowing both to mine Howland’s guano deposits.[4] Guano was mined by American and British interests, and the island’s guano-mining enterprises ended in 1878 after the deposits were depleted.[3][4]

In 1935, colonists from Hawaii arrived as part of the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panalaʻau Project, which was intended to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] The initial group consisted of four male alumni and students from Kamehameha School in Honolulu, who had signed on as a scientific expedition and were expected to collect botanical and biological specimens during rotating assignments.[4] The settlers established Itascatown, named for the U.S. Coast Guard cutter Itasca, and made regular trips among the other American equatorial island settlements.[4] Howland was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936, and facilities on the island were prepared for use as an air stop on flights between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

On July 2, 1937, aviator Amelia Earhart and navigator Frederick J. Noonan disappeared near Howland while attempting to reach the island after departing Lae, New Guinea, during Earhart’s around-the-world flight attempt.[1][2] During World War II, the United States used Howland as a military lookout point.[1] After its period of occupation and exploitation, Howland came to be managed for wildlife conservation as part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system and was included in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009.[2][3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974, protecting the island’s land, surrounding waters, and submerged and emergent lands from the mean low-water line out to 3 nautical miles.[3] Following the 2009 creation of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, the refuge boundary was extended to 12 nautical miles, while monument waters and submerged and emergent lands out to 50 nautical miles are cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and NOAA.[3] The refuge is part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge System and provides nesting areas and foraging habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and other marine wildlife.[1][2] Its marine environment includes an active fringing reef around the coral-fragment island, with reef imagery used for mapping, ecosystem assessment, and bathymetric studies of the surrounding seafloor.[2] Refuge management priorities include biological surveys, habitat assessment, scientific data collection, additional marine habitat exploration, and projects intended to attract rare seabird species.[5] Federal planning documents for the remote Pacific refuges proposed no public uses and identified opportunities for the refuges to serve as baseline sites for global climate-change research under more intensive management alternatives.[5] Conservation concerns include the effects of past invasive species, including Polynesian rats introduced in the nineteenth century and eradicated in 1983, as well as broader threats to coral reefs from global warming and ocean acidification.[3] The protected waters are notable among surveyed U.S. Pacific islands for high fish biomass and for occurrences of Endangered Species Act-listed wildlife, including high observed densities of green sea turtles.[3]

==Earhart Light==
A small lighthouse, later known as Earhart Light, was built on Howland Island in 1937 in connection with preparations for Amelia Earhart’s planned refueling stop there during her world flight.[4][2] Earhart never reached the island, and the structure subsequently became associated with her disappearance as a memorial beacon on the island she had been trying to reach.[4][3]

During World War II, Japanese attacks on Howland damaged the lighthouse; aircraft strafed it, shattered a plaque honoring Earhart, and shelling left the beacon unusable.[5][3] The station has been inactive since 1942, and the remaining structure has since been regarded as a day beacon rather than an operating lighthouse.[4][5] The lighthouse was partially destroyed during the war and rebuilt in the early 1960s, with a 1962 U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service photograph documenting the Amelia Earhart light beacon on the island.[2][3] Later visits included restoration work and the addition of the names of the two colonists killed during the wartime attack.[3]

==Image gallery==
<gallery>
File:ISS010-E-9287 - View of Howland Island.jpg|Howland Island photographed from the International Space Station on 3 December 2004 during Expedition 10.[1][3]
File:Howland island nasa.jpg|NASA astronaut image ISS010-E-09287 was taken with a Kodak DCS760C electronic still camera and an 800 mm lens from an altitude of 192 nautical miles (356 km).[1][5]
File:ISS010-E-9287 - View of Howland Island.jpg|White breakers around the island mark the position of the surrounding reef in the astronaut image.[1][3]
</gallery>
